title: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae
section: Protozoal and Helminthic Infections General Considerations
authors: Rosa M. Andrade, Sharon L. Reed

items.subtopic: Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION
items.title: Understanding the Spectrum of Amebiasis
items.content: Amebiasis is an infection caused by Entamoeba histolytica, ranging from asymptomatic colonization to invasive disease. Most cases are asymptomatic (90%), while 10% develop invasive amebiasis, often presenting as intestinal colitis or liver abscesses.
keyPoints: Infection spectrum: 90% asymptomatic colonization, 10% invasive amebiasis
Common presentations of invasive disease: Intestinal colitis (dysentery/diarrhea) and liver abscesses
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION
items.title: Transmission Pathways of E. histolytica
items.content: E. histolytica is acquired through ingestion of cysts from contaminated sources, primarily food-borne. Transmission can also occur via oral-anal practices or colonic irrigation devices.
keyPoints: Primary transmission: Ingestion of viable cysts from fecally contaminated water, food, or hands
Less common routes: Oral and anal sexual practices, direct rectal inoculation
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Epidemiology of E. histolytica Infections
items.content: E. histolytica predominantly affects underdeveloped regions, particularly affecting children <5 years old. It is also a concern for travelers and specific groups in developed countries.
keyPoints: High prevalence in tropical regions with poor sanitation (e.g., Indian subcontinent, Africa, parts of East Asia, Central/South America)
Global burden: 26,748 deaths annually; 4,567 children <5 years old
At-risk groups in developed countries: Travelers, recent immigrants, MSM, military personnel, institutional inmates
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Mechanisms of Tissue Invasion by E. histolytica Trophozoites
items.content: E. histolytica trophozoites invade tissue through mechanisms involving Gal/GalNAc adherence lectin, which facilitates attachment to colonic mucus and epithelial cells. They release glycosidases and proteases that degrade mucus polymers and extracellular cysteine proteinases that degrade collagen, elastin, IgA, IgG, and anaphylatoxins C3a and C5a. Trophozoites cause mucosal damage through contact-dependent cytotoxicity (apoptosis induction, trogocytosis, lysis of inflammatory cells) and contact-independent cytotoxicity via inflammatory mediators like prostaglandin E2.
keyPoints: Trophozoite size: 20–60 μm in diameter
Virulence factors include Gal/GalNAc adherence lectin, glycosidases, proteases, cysteine proteinases
Contact-dependent cytotoxicity involves apoptosis induction and lysis of inflammatory cells
Contact-independent cytotoxicity is mediated by prostaglandin E2
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Role of Antioxidant Systems in E. histolytica Virulence
items.content: E. histolytica's resistance to reactive oxygen and nitrogen species is a virulence factor, relying on its thioredoxin–thioredoxin reductase system due to the absence of glutathione and glutathione reductase. This system reduces reactive nitrogen species and uses alternative electron donors like NAD. Metronidazole targets this antioxidant system to exert its antiparasitic effect.
keyPoints: E. histolytica lacks glutathione and glutathione reductase
Relies on thioredoxin–thioredoxin reductase for oxidative stress resistance
Metronidazole inhibits the antioxidant system
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Gut Microbiome on E. histolytica Pathogenesis
items.content: The gut microbiome influences E. histolytica pathogenesis, with a diverse microbiome in asymptomatic carriers showing high abundance of Ruminococcaceae, Coriobacteriaceae, and Clostridiaceae. In contrast, patients with invasive disease have low or absent levels of these bacteria. Fecal anti-Gal/GalNAc lectin IgA is protective against reinfection, while a high parasite burden in early life correlates with increased Prevotella copri.
keyPoints: Diverse microbiome in asymptomatic carriers includes Ruminococcaceae, Coriobacteriaceae, Clostridiaceae
Low diversity associated with invasive disease
Fecal anti-Gal/GalNAc lectin IgA reduces reinfection risk by 64%
High parasite burden correlates with increased Prevotella copri
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Susceptibility in E. histolytica Infection
items.content: Certain genetic variations influence susceptibility to amebic diseases, with specific alleles and mutations affecting disease risk and progression.
keyPoints: Heterozygosity of MHC class II allele DQB1*0601 is protective against intestinal disease, highlighting the role of CD4+ T cells in resistance.
A single amino acid substitution (Q223R) in LEPRs quadruples risk for amebic intestinal disease in children and increases risk for liver abscesses in adults.
Variations in CREM/CUL2 locus increase diarrhea risk in children acquiring E. histolytica within the first year of life.
Both Q223R and CREM variations are overrepresented in certain geographical regions.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Pathology of Amebic Liver Abscess
items.content: Amebic liver abscesses have distinct age and gender predilections, with pathophysiological mechanisms involving immune modulation and tissue invasion.
keyPoints: Men aged 30–60 are most commonly affected by amebic liver abscesses at a rate 10-12 times higher than women in the same age group.
Testosterone may increase susceptibility to liver abscesses by affecting interferon γ secretion via natural killer T cells.
Amebic liver abscesses follow intestinal colonization, often asymptomatic, and involve trophozoite invasion of veins through the portal system.
E. histolytica is resistant to complement-mediated lysis, aiding its survival in the bloodstream.
Liver parenchyma becomes necrotic with a thin rim of congested tissue; abscess contents are described as 'anchovy paste' but can vary in color.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Amebic Dysentery
items.content: Amebic dysentery presents with lower abdominal pain, mild diarrhea progressing to malaise, weight loss, and heme-positive stools. Unlike bacterial dysentery, fewer than 40% of patients are febrile.
keyPoints: Symptomatic colitis develops 2–6 weeks post-ingestion
<40% of amebic dysentery cases are febrile
Stools contain blood and mucus with minimal fecal material
Differential diagnosis includes acute appendicitis
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · CLINICAL SYNDROMES

items.title: Recognition of Amebic Liver Abscess in Travelers
items.content: Amebic liver abscess is common among travelers returning from endemic areas, with 95% developing symptoms within 5 months. Young patients often present acutely with fever and right-upper-quadrant pain.
keyPoints: 95% of traveler cases develop within 5 months
Younger patients show acute symptoms (<10 days)
Common symptoms: fever, right-upper-quadrant pain, pleural effusion
Consider in differential diagnosis for fever of unknown origin
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · CLINICAL SYNDROMES

items.title: Complications and Management of Amebic Liver Abscess
items.content: Amebic liver abscess can lead to pleuropulmonary involvement in 20–30% of cases, requiring drainage if rupture into the pleural space occurs. Hepatobronchial fistulae are dramatic but have a good prognosis.
keyPoints: Pleuropulmonary involvement occurs in 20-30%
Rupture into pleural space requires drainage
Hepatobronchial fistulas carry a good prognosis
Pericardium rupture is grave and needs surgical intervention
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · CLINICAL SYNDROMES

items.title: Importance of Multiple Stool Examinations in Amebic Colitis
items.content: The diagnosis of amebic colitis relies heavily on stool examinations, which should include wet mounts, iodine-stained concentrates, and trichrome-stained preparations. These tests confirm the diagnosis in 75–95% of cases when examining at least three fresh stool specimens.
keyPoints: Examine at least three fresh stool specimens for accurate diagnosis.
Combination of wet mounts, iodine-stained concentrates, and trichrome-stained preparations confirms diagnosis in 75–95% of cases.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.title: Role of Serology in Diagnosing Invasive Amebiasis
items.content: Serologic tests, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays, are crucial for diagnosing invasive amebiasis. They are positive in over 90% of cases with colitis, ameboma, or liver abscess and help assess the risk in asymptomatic individuals.
keyPoints: Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays positive in >90% of cases.
Serologic findings usually revert to negative within 6–12 months.
Helpful for assessing risk in asymptomatic, cyst-passing individuals.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.title: Advantages of Rapid Diagnostic Tests for E. histolytica
items.content: Enzyme immunoassays and PCR tests offer rapid, sensitive, and specific detection of E. histolytica. Enzyme immunoassays have a sensitivity of 87% and specificity >90%, while PCR approaches 100% sensitivity and specificity.
keyPoints: Enzyme immunoassays: Sensitivity 87%, Specificity >90%.
PCR tests: Sensitivity and specificity approach 100%.
Results available in as little as an hour.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.title: Radiographic Techniques in Amebic Liver Abscess Detection
items.content: Ultrasonography, CT, and MRI are valuable for identifying hypoechoic cysts indicative of amebic liver abscesses. The location and number of abscesses vary with symptom duration: over 80% of patients symptomatic for more than 10 days have a single right lobe abscess, while about 50% of those symptomatic for less than 10 days present multiple abscesses.
keyPoints: Ultrasonography, CT, MRI detect hypoechoic cysts in amebic liver abscesses.
>80% with symptoms >10 days have a single right lobe abscess.
~50% with symptoms <10 days have multiple abscesses.
Complications include large (>10 cm) abscesses in the superior part of the right lobe, potential rupture into pleural space or pericardium for left lobe lesions.
Complete resolution within 6 months expected in two-thirds; 10% may persist a year.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2)

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Amebic Liver Abscess
items.content: Amebic liver abscesses can mimic other conditions such as pulmonary or gallbladder disease, malaria, or typhoid fever. Differentiation from pyogenic abscess is crucial; patients with pyogenic abscesses are often older and have underlying bowel disease or recent surgery history. Amebic serology aids diagnosis, but aspiration for Gram's staining and culture may be necessary.
keyPoints: Differential includes pulmonary/gallbladder diseases, malaria, typhoid fever.
Consider in high-risk groups with travel outside the US or institutional inmates.
Differentiate amebic from pyogenic abscess; older patients with bowel disease/surgery history may indicate pyogenic.
Amebic serology useful; aspiration for Gram's stain and culture may be needed.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2)

items.title: Treatment Strategy for Amebic Liver Abscess
items.content: Metronidazole is the primary treatment for amebic liver abscess, with tinidazole and ornidazole being effective as single-dose therapies in developing countries. Early diagnosis and therapy result in mortality rates of less than 1%. All patients should receive a luminal agent to eradicate cysts.
keyPoints: Metronidazole is the drug of choice for amebic liver abscess.
Tinidazole and ornidazole can be used as single-dose therapies in developing countries.
Mortality rates from uncomplicated amebic liver abscess are <1% with early diagnosis and therapy.
All treatment regimens should include a luminal agent to eradicate cysts and prevent further transmission.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · INTESTINAL DISEASE (TABLE 230-1) | AMEBIC LIVER ABSCESS

items.title: Indications for Aspiration of Amebic Liver Abscesses
items.content: Aspiration is indicated to rule out pyogenic abscess, lack of clinical response in 3–5 days, threat of imminent rupture, and prevention of rupture into the pericardium. Percutaneous drainage may be successful even if the abscess has already ruptured.
keyPoints: Indications for aspiration include ruling out pyogenic abscess, lack of clinical response in 3–5 days, threat of imminent rupture, and prevention of rupture into the pericardium.
Aspiration may be necessary for multiple lesions or left-lobe abscesses threatening to rupture into the pericardium.
Percutaneous drainage can be successful even if the liver abscess has already ruptured.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · INTESTINAL DISEASE (TABLE 230-1) | AMEBIC LIVER ABSCESS

items.title: Prevention Strategies for Amebic Infection
items.content: Amebic infection is primarily spread through ingestion of cyst-contaminated food or water. Effective prevention involves ensuring adequate sanitation and reducing cyst carriage, as asymptomatic carriers can excrete up to 15 million cysts per day. In high-risk areas, avoiding unpeeled fruits and vegetables and using bottled water are recommended preventive measures.
keyPoints: Ingestion of food or water contaminated with cysts spreads amebic infection.
Asymptomatic carriers may excrete up to 15 million cysts per day.
Prevention requires adequate sanitation and eradication of cyst carriage.
Avoid unpeeled fruits and vegetables and use bottled water in high-risk areas.
Cysts are resistant to chlorine; iodination (tetraglycine hydroperiodide) is recommended for disinfection.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PREVENTION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Epidemiology of Free-Living Amebae Infections
items.content: The major human pathogens among free-living amebae are Acanthamoeba, Naegleria, and Balamuthia. These organisms can cause severe CNS infections that are often fatal. They are found worldwide in various water sources and soil. Recent cases have highlighted new risk factors such as exposure to piped water for Naegleria fowleri and organ transplantation for Balamuthia mandrillaris.
keyPoints: Major human pathogens include Acanthamoeba, Naegleria, and Balamuthia.
These amebae can cause serious, often fatal CNS infections.
Acanthamoeba and Naegleria are found in diverse water sources worldwide.
Balamuthia is primarily associated with soil.
New risk factors: piped water exposure for Naegleria fowleri; organ transplantation for Balamuthia mandrillaris.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PREVENTION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Understanding Primary Amebic Meningoencephalitis (PAM) Epidemiology and Risk Factors
items.content: Primary amebic meningoencephalitis is a rare, but fulminant CNS infection caused by Naegleria fowleri. It predominantly affects young males with a median age of 14 years. Most cases are linked to exposure in warm freshwater environments, particularly during summer months.
keyPoints: 157 U.S. cases reported from 1962-2022; 381 worldwide
Cases stable annually (0–8), rising in northern states due to climate change
Affects healthy males predominantly (75%) with median age of 14 years
91% exposure linked to recreational freshwater activities
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · NAEGLERIA INFECTIONS

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnostic Approach for PAM
items.content: PAM presents after an incubation period with severe headache, high fever, nausea, vomiting, and meningismus. Diagnosis is suggested by purulent meningitis without bacterial evidence on Gram's stain; CSF shows elevated WBCs (up to 20,000/μL), protein, low glucose, and motile trophozoites in wet mounts.
keyPoints: Incubation period: 2–15 days
Symptoms: severe headache, high fever, nausea, vomiting, meningismus
CSF findings: WBCs up to 20,000/μL, elevated protein, low glucose
Diagnosis via motile trophozoites in CSF wet mounts
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · NAEGLERIA INFECTIONS

items.title: Treatment Strategies and Prognosis for PAM
items.content: PAM has a poor prognosis with most patients dying within a week. Treatment involves combination therapy including amphotericin B, fluconazole, azithromycin, rifampin, dexamethasone, and miltefosine. Early diagnosis and aggressive management are crucial for better outcomes.
keyPoints: Prognosis: most patients die within a week
Survivors treated with amphotericin B, fluconazole, azithromycin, rifampin, dexamethasone, miltefosine
Miltefosine is crucial in treatment regimens for survivors
Contact CDC for PCR diagnosis and treatment recommendations
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · NAEGLERIA INFECTIONS

items.title: Granulomatous Amebic Encephalitis: Recognizing and Diagnosing CNS Infection
items.content: Granulomatous amebic encephalitis, caused by at least eight different Acanthamoeba species, presents insidiously in chronically ill or debilitated patients. It mimics space-occupying lesions with symptoms like altered mental status, headache, stiff neck, and focal neurological deficits.
keyPoints: Occurs more indolently than Naegleria infection
Risk factors: lymphoproliferative disorders, chemotherapy, glucocorticoid therapy, lupus erythematosus, post-transplant, AIDS
CNS involvement often mimics space-occupying lesions
Symptoms include altered mental status, headache, stiff neck, cranial nerve palsies, ataxia, hemiparesis
Cutaneous ulcers or nodules may be present in disseminated infection
CSF examination for trophozoites is helpful; lumbar puncture contraindicated with increased intracerebral pressure
CT shows cortical/subcortical lesions of decreased density, mimicking embolic infarcts or toxoplasmosis
Diagnosis: Demonstration of trophozoites/cysts in specimens, culture on nonnutrient agar with E. coli, real-time PCR assay available from CDC
Survival for AIDS/transplant recipients is 3–40 days due to difficulty forming granulomas
Treatment includes miltefosine as part of combination therapy
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · ☐ ACANTHAMOEBA INFECTIONS

items.title: Acanthamoeba Keratitis: Identification and Management
items.content: The incidence of Acanthamoeba keratitis has increased, primarily linked to extended-wear contact lenses. Symptoms include tearing and foreign body sensation, progressing rapidly to a characteristic annular corneal ring.
keyPoints: Increased incidence over the past 20 years due to improved diagnosis
Linked to extended-wear contact lenses, LASIK, homemade saline, swimming with lenses, inadequate disinfection
Initial symptoms: tearing, foreign body sensation
Characteristic sign: annular paracentral corneal ring (corneal abscess)
Differential diagnosis: bacterial, mycobacterial, herpetic infection
Diagnosis: Identification of Acanthamoeba cysts in corneal scrapings/biopsy, growth on special media
Cysts are drug-resistant; medical therapy often disappointing
Partial responses reported with polyhexamethylene biguanide (0.2%) or propamidine isethionate (0.1%) eyedrops
Severe cases may require keratoplasty
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · ☐ ACANTHAMOEBA INFECTIONS

items.title: Transmission and Risk Factors of Balamuthia mandrillaris Infections
items.content: Balamuthia mandrillaris is a free-living ameba causing granulomatous amebic encephalitis, cutaneous lesions, and sinus infections. It spreads through percutaneous or mucosal exposure and can be transmitted hematogenously to the brain and other organs, including via organ transplantation. Recognized risk factors include young age, immunocompromising conditions, and Hispanic ethnicity.
keyPoints: First identified in 1986 from a mandrill baboon
>100 cases reported in the United States; >200 worldwide
Isolated from soil and dust
Risk factors: young age, immunocompromising conditions, Hispanic ethnicity
Transmission through organ transplantation with an incubation period of 17–24 days
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · BALAMUTHIA INFECTIONS

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Balamuthia mandrillaris Infections
items.content: Balamuthia infections are diagnosed through CSF analysis showing mononuclear or neutrophilic pleocytosis, elevated protein levels, normal to low glucose concentrations. Imaging typically reveals multiple hypodense lesions. Diagnosis can be confirmed with multiplex PCR from the CDC and serology via fluorescent antibodies. Treatment involves a combination of drugs including pentamidine, flucytosine, sulfadiazine, macrolides, and miltefosine.
keyPoints: CSF analysis: mononuclear or neutrophilic pleocytosis, elevated protein, normal to low glucose
Imaging reveals multiple hypodense lesions
Differential diagnosis includes tuberculomas and neurocysticercosis
Multiplex PCR test from the CDC available for testing
Treatment options: pentamidine, flucytosine, sulfadiazine, macrolides, miltefosine
Contact CDC Emergency Operations Center at (770)-488-7100 for assistance
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · BALAMUTHIA INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: Which of the following is a common clinical presentation of invasive amebiasis?
items.explanation: Invasive amebiasis frequently presents as intestinal colitis (dysentery or diarrhea) or as extraintestinal amebiasis, in which abscesses of the liver are more commonly found.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: What is the most prevalent form of transmission for E. histolytica?
items.explanation: Food-borne exposure is the most prevalent form of transmission for E. histolytica.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: Which groups are at risk for amebiasis in developed countries?
items.explanation: In developed countries, returning travelers, recent immigrants, men who have sex with men (MSM), military personnel, and inmates are at risk for amebiasis.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: Which species of Entamoeba is genetically distinct from E. dispar?
items.explanation: E. histolytica has unique surface antigens, is genetically distinct, and possesses virulence properties that distinguish it from E. dispar.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which enzyme system does E. histolytica rely on to counteract oxidative stress, given its lack of glutathione and glutathione reductase?
items.explanation: E. histolytica relies on the thioredoxin–thioredoxin reductase system to manage oxidative stress due to its lack of glutathione and glutathione reductase.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: What is a consequence of E. histolytica trophozoites phagocytosing intestinal bacteria?
options: Enhanced virulence due to increased nutrient availability
items.explanation: Phagocytosis is a virulence factor, and inhibition leads to defective proliferation of E. histolytica.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: What role does IgA play in acquired immunity against E. histolytica?
options: It is not protective and correlates with the duration of illness
It reduces the risk of new infection by targeting Gal/GalNAc
items.explanation: IgA, specifically intestinal IgA responding to Gal/GalNAc, reduces the risk of new E. histolytica infection.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which bacteria are associated with a more homogeneous gut microbiome in asymptomatic E. histolytica carriers?
items.explanation: Asymptomatic carriers of E. histolytica have a more homogeneous gut microbiome with high abundance of Ruminococcaceae, Coriobacteriaceae, and Clostridiaceae.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which genetic variation nearly quadrupled the risk for amebic intestinal disease in children and increased the risk for amebic liver abscesses in adults?
options: Heterozygosity of MHC class II allele DQB1 *0601
Single amino acid substitution Q223R in LEPRs
Overrepresentation of genetic variations in the geographical region
items.explanation: The single amino acid substitution (Q223R) in LEPRs nearly quadrupled the risk for amebic intestinal disease in children and increased the risk for amebic liver abscesses in adults.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: What is the typical appearance of treated ulcers in amebiasis?
options: Characteristically heal with little or no scarring
Always result in full-thickness necrosis and perforation
Lead to exuberant formation of granulation tissue
items.explanation: Treated ulcers characteristically heal with little or no scarring.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: What is the role of testosterone in amebic liver abscesses?
options: Decreases susceptibility by reducing interferon γ secretion
Increases susceptibility by modulating interferon γ secretion
Has no effect on susceptibility to amebic liver abscesses
Prevents E. histolytica from invading the liver
items.explanation: Testosterone may increase susceptibility to amebic liver abscess by modulating the secretion of interferon γ.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Which percentage of patients with amebic dysentery are typically febrile?
items.explanation: Fewer than 40% of patients with amebic dysentery are febrile.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · CLINICAL SYNDROMES

items.question: What is the most common type of amebic infection?
items.explanation: The most common type of amebic infection is asymptomatic cyst passage.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · CLINICAL SYNDROMES

items.question: Which complication of an amebic liver abscess carries the gravest prognosis?
items.explanation: Rupture into the pericardium carries the gravest prognosis.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · CLINICAL SYNDROMES

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of stools in patients with amebic dysentery?
items.explanation: Stools contain little fecal material and consist mainly of blood and mucus.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · CLINICAL SYNDROMES

items.question: Which of the following findings in stool examinations are suggestive of amebic colitis?
options: Positive test for heme, a paucity of neutrophils, and amebic cysts or trophozoites
Presence of Entamoeba coli and Endolimax nana
Negative serologic findings in acute cases
items.explanation: A positive test for heme, a paucity of neutrophils, and the presence of amebic cysts or trophozoites are indicative of amebic colitis.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.question: What is required to definitively diagnose amebic colitis?
options: Detection of Entamoeba trophozoites that have ingested erythrocytes
items.explanation: The definitive diagnosis requires the demonstration of hematophagous trophozoites of E. histolytica.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.question: Why is it important to examine multiple stool specimens when diagnosing amebiasis?
options: Trophozoites are killed rapidly by water, drying, or barium
Cultures of amebae are not sensitive enough
Serologic tests are unreliable in early infection
items.explanation: Trophozoites are killed rapidly by water, drying, or barium, necessitating the examination of multiple fresh stool specimens.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.question: Which diagnostic test is particularly useful for assessing the risk of invasive amebiasis in asymptomatic individuals?
items.explanation: Serologic tests are helpful in assessing the risk of invasive amebiasis in asymptomatic, cyst-passing individuals.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging technique is useful for detecting a hypoechoic cyst in patients with amebic liver abscess?
items.explanation: Ultrasonography, CT, and MRI are useful for detecting hypoechoic cysts in amebic liver abscess.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of patients with symptoms lasting more than 10 days typically have a single liver abscess?
items.explanation: More than 80% of patients who have had symptoms for >10 days have a single abscess.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2)

items.question: Which diagnostic test is helpful in differentiating amebic from pyogenic liver abscess?
items.explanation: Amebic serology is helpful, but aspiration may be required for differentiation.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2)

items.question: Which luminal amebicide is available in the United States for treating asymptomatic carriers of E. histolytica?
items.explanation: Both iodoquinol and paromomycin are luminal amebicides available in the United States.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · INTESTINAL DISEASE (TABLE 230-1) | AMEBIC LIVER ABSCESS

items.question: What is the primary reason for administering a luminal agent after treating with metronidazole for amebic liver abscess?
options: To treat side effects of metronidazole
To eradicate cysts in the bowel
items.explanation: Metronidazole does not eradicate cysts, so a luminal agent is needed to do so.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · INTESTINAL DISEASE (TABLE 230-1) | AMEBIC LIVER ABSCESS

items.question: What is the mortality rate for uncomplicated amebic liver abscess with early diagnosis and therapy?
items.explanation: Early diagnosis and therapy result in a mortality rate of less than 1%.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · INTESTINAL DISEASE (TABLE 230-1) | AMEBIC LIVER ABSCESS

items.question: Which method is recommended for disinfecting water to prevent amebic infection due to cyst resistance?
items.explanation: Cysts are resistant to chlorine, so iodination is recommended.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PREVENTION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is a common preventive measure in high-risk areas for amebic infection?
options: Avoidance of unpeeled fruits and vegetables
items.explanation: In high-risk areas, avoiding unpeeled fruits and vegetables helps prevent infection.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PREVENTION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a major human pathogen among free-living amebae?
items.explanation: Entamoeba is not listed as a major human pathogen among free-living amebae.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PREVENTION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is a common route through which Naegleria fowleri enters the central nervous system (CNS)?
options: Inhalation of contaminated water or dust leading to entry via the olfactory neuroepithelium.
Through open wounds in the skin.
Via respiratory droplets from an infected person.
items.explanation: N. fowleri trophozoites enter the CNS through the olfactory neuroepithelium following inhalation of water or dust contaminated with trophozoites or cysts.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · NAEGLERIA INFECTIONS

items.question: What is a common clinical presentation in patients with primary amebic meningoencephalitis (PAM) caused by Naegleria fowleri?
options: Severe headache, high fever, nausea, vomiting, and meningismus.
Chronic cough and shortness of breath.
items.explanation: Patients with PAM typically develop severe headache, high fever, nausea, vomiting, and meningismus after an incubation period of 2–15 days.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · NAEGLERIA INFECTIONS

items.question: Which diagnostic method is NOT useful for acute Naegleria fowleri infection due to the presence of antibodies in healthy adults?
options: Detection of motile trophozoites in wet mounts of fresh spinal fluid.
items.explanation: Serologic testing is not useful for acute Naegleria infection because antibodies to Naegleria species have been detected in healthy adults.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · NAEGLERIA INFECTIONS

items.question: Which of the following treatments has NOT been included in all seven survivors of reported worldwide cases of PAM?
items.explanation: All seven survivors included miltefosine and a combination of amphotericin B, fluconazole, azithromycin, rifampin, and dexamethasone; penicillin is not mentioned as part of the treatment.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · NAEGLERIA INFECTIONS

items.question: Which of the following conditions typically mimics a space-occupying lesion in patients with Granulomatous Amebic Encephalitis?
items.explanation: In the CNS, Granulomatous Amebic Encephalitis often mimics a space-occupying lesion.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · ☐ ACANTHAMOEBA INFECTIONS

items.question: Which diagnostic method is contraindicated in patients with increased intracerebral pressure due to Granulomatous Amebic Encephalitis?
items.explanation: Lumbar puncture may be contraindicated because of increased intracerebral pressure.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · ☐ ACANTHAMOEBA INFECTIONS

items.question: What is the primary risk factor for Acanthamoeba keratitis in recent years?
items.explanation: Most infections are linked to extended-wear contact lenses.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · ☐ ACANTHAMOEBA INFECTIONS

items.question: Which of the following is a potential risk factor for granulomatous amebic encephalitis caused by Balamuthia mandrillaris?
options: Young age, immunocompromising conditions, and Hispanic ethnicity
Older age and African American ethnicity
Middle-aged adults with no known health issues
items.explanation: The passage identifies young age, immunocompromising conditions, and Hispanic ethnicity as risk factors.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · BALAMUTHIA INFECTIONS

items.question: What is the typical incubation period for organ transplant-transmitted B. mandrillaris infections?
items.explanation: The passage states that the incubation period for organ transplant-transmitted B. mandrillaris infections is 17–24 days.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · BALAMUTHIA INFECTIONS

items.question: Which diagnostic method involves examination of CSF to reveal mononuclear or neutrophilic pleocytosis, elevated protein levels, and normal to low glucose concentrations?
options: Multiplex PCR test from the CDC
items.explanation: The passage specifies that examination of CSF reveals these characteristics in diagnosing Balamuthia infections.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · BALAMUTHIA INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: E. histolytica trophozoites can survive exposure to air.
items.explanation: Trophozoites are rapidly killed by exposure to air.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: E. histolytica is the second most common cause of death related to parasitic infection worldwide.
items.explanation: Worldwide, E. histolytica is the second most common cause of death related to parasitic infection (after malaria).
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: E. histolytica trophozoites are capable of resisting reactive oxygen species through their glutathione system.
items.explanation: E. histolytica lacks glutathione and relies on the thioredoxin–thioredoxin reductase system to resist reactive oxygen species.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: Passive immunity from maternal parasite-specific IgA reduces the risk of E. histolytica infection in infants.
items.explanation: Maternal parasite-specific IgA via breastfeeding results in a reduced risk of E. histolytica infection and diarrheal disease in infants.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: Human intestinal infection with E. histolytica is marked by a significant presence of inflammatory cells.
items.explanation: Human intestinal infection is marked by a paucity of inflammatory cells.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 2)

items.statement: Amebic liver abscesses are always preceded by symptomatic intestinal colonization.
items.explanation: Liver abscesses are always preceded by intestinal colonization, which may be asymptomatic.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 2)

items.statement: Most patients with symptomatic amebic colitis develop symptoms within a week after ingestion of infectious cysts.
items.explanation: Symptomatic amebic colitis develops 2–6 weeks after the ingestion of infectious cysts.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · CLINICAL SYNDROMES

items.statement: Amebic liver abscesses often present with jaundice.
items.explanation: Jaundice is rare in patients with an amebic liver abscess.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · CLINICAL SYNDROMES

items.statement: The cysts of E. histolytica can be distinguished microscopically from those of E. dispar.
items.explanation: Cysts of E. histolytica cannot be distinguished microscopically from those of E. dispar.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.statement: Enzyme immunoassays using monoclonal antibodies for the Gal/GalNAc lectin have a sensitivity of 87% and specificity >90% in stool.
items.explanation: These enzyme immunoassays are indeed specific for detecting E. histolytica with high sensitivity and specificity.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.statement: Complete resolution of a liver abscess within 6 months can be anticipated in all patients.
items.explanation: Two-thirds of patients may see complete resolution within 6 months, but 10% may have persistent abnormalities for a year.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2)

items.statement: Amebic liver abscess can be confused with pulmonary or gallbladder disease.
items.explanation: Due to varied symptoms, amebic liver abscess may mimic other conditions like pulmonary or gallbladder disease.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2)

items.statement: Metronidazole resistance has been found in clinical isolates.
items.explanation: Resistance to metronidazole has not been found in clinical isolates.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · INTESTINAL DISEASE (TABLE 230-1) | AMEBIC LIVER ABSCESS

items.statement: Aspiration of liver abscesses accelerates healing.
items.explanation: There is no evidence that aspiration accelerates healing.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · INTESTINAL DISEASE (TABLE 230-1) | AMEBIC LIVER ABSCESS

items.statement: There is an effective prophylaxis for preventing amebic infection.
items.explanation: The passage states there is no effective prophylaxis.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PREVENTION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Acanthamoeba and Naegleria can be isolated from hospital water networks.
items.explanation: The passage confirms these amebae have been isolated from hospital water networks.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PREVENTION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: The prognosis for patients with primary amebic meningoencephalitis (PAM) is generally poor, with most dying within a week.
items.explanation: Most patients die within a week due to the rapid progression of PAM.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · NAEGLERIA INFECTIONS

items.statement: Transplantation of solid organs from PAM patients has been associated with transmission of N. fowleri.
items.explanation: Despite evidence of extra-CNS dissemination, transplantation of their solid organs have not been associated with PAM in transplant recipients.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · NAEGLERIA INFECTIONS

items.statement: Acanthamoeba cysts are resistant to available drugs.
items.explanation: Cysts of Acanthamoeba are resistant to available drugs, making treatment challenging.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · ☐ ACANTHAMOEBA INFECTIONS

items.statement: Granulomatous amebic encephalitis has a more rapid course in patients with AIDS or transplant recipients.
items.explanation: These individuals have difficulty forming granulomas, leading to an accelerated disease course.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · ☐ ACANTHAMOEBA INFECTIONS

items.statement: Balamuthia mandrillaris can be transmitted through organ transplantation.
items.explanation: The passage mentions clusters of infections detected in multiple recipients from the same donor, indicating transmission via organ transplantation.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · BALAMUTHIA INFECTIONS

items.statement: Balamuthia mandrillaris is only found in immunocompromised individuals.
items.explanation: The passage states that Balamuthia often affects immunocompetent individuals as well.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · BALAMUTHIA INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.assertion: E. dispar is incapable of causing invasive disease.
items.reason: Most asymptomatic carriers, including MSM and patients with AIDS, harbor E. dispar and have self-limited infections.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why E. dispar is incapable of causing invasive disease.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.assertion: E. histolytica infections are more common among women than men.
items.reason: Invasive colitis and liver abscesses are tenfold more common among men due to differences in complement-mediated killing and effects of testosterone on interferon γ secretion.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because invasive colitis and liver abscesses are more common among men than women.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · Amebiasis and Infection with Free-Living Amebae | DEFINITION | LIFE CYCLE AND TRANSMISSION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: E. histolytica trophozoites cause tissue damage by contact-dependent cytotoxicity.
items.reason: This is due to the induction of apoptotic cell death and lysis of inflammatory cells through release of phospholipase A and pore-forming peptides.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the mechanism of contact-dependent cytotoxicity.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Serum IgG antibodies protect against E. histolytica infection.
items.reason: IgG antibody titers correlate with the severity of disease and provide immunity.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; serum IgG does not protect against E. histolytica, though it correlates with illness duration.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Environmental enteropathy is linked to malnutrition and stunted growth.
items.reason: It is associated with functional gastrointestinal impairment causing malnutrition and stunted growth in children within the first 2 years of life.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · CLINICAL SYNDROMES

items.assertion: Patients with amebic liver abscess always present with active diarrhea.
items.reason: The initial site of infection is the colon.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; fewer than one-third of patients have active diarrhea.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · CLINICAL SYNDROMES

items.assertion: Serologic tests should be performed in patients with ulcerative colitis before glucocorticoid therapy.
items.reason: To prevent the development of severe colitis or toxic megacolon owing to unsuspected amebiasis.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why serologic tests are recommended prior to glucocorticoid therapy in ulcerative colitis patients.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.assertion: Routine hematology and chemistry tests are very helpful in diagnosing invasive amebiasis.
items.reason: About three-fourths of patients with an amebic liver abscess have leukocytosis (>10,000 cells/μL).
items.explanation: Routine hematology and chemistry tests are not very helpful in diagnosing invasive amebiasis despite the presence of leukocytosis.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.assertion: Amebic serology is the definitive test for diagnosing amebic liver abscess.
items.reason: Aspiration of the abscess with Gram's staining and culture may be required to differentiate from pyogenic abscess.
items.explanation: Both are true, but aspiration might still be needed; serology alone is not definitive.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2)

items.assertion: The diagnosis of Naegleria infection should be considered in patients with purulent meningitis without evidence of bacteria.
items.reason: Other laboratory findings resemble those for fulminant bacterial meningitis, including elevated intracranial pressure and high white blood cell counts.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · NAEGLERIA INFECTIONS

items.assertion: Miltefosine has been included in the treatment regimen for all survivors of PAM.
items.reason: Miltefosine is a new antiparasitic agent active against Naegleria fowleri and other amebae.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why miltefosine has been included in treatment for all survivors.
items.reference: Amebiasis and Infection with Free Living Amebae · NAEGLERIA INFECTIONS
